answer:
Iqbal's poem "Swami Ram Tirath," from *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, eulogizes the Hindu mystic Swami Ram Tirath (1873–1906) as a figure who achieved profound spiritual annihilation (*fana*) and transcendence, merging with the divine like a restless drop dissolving into the river to become a priceless pearl.[^1] The poet marvels at how the mystic gracefully pierced beyond sensory illusions ("color and scent") to reveal ultimate unity, while the speaker remains trapped in distinctions.[^2] 

It portrays his death as a triumphant transformation: his annihilation turns life's noise into the clamor of Judgment Day, and his extinguished spark becomes Azar's fire-temple (a Zoroastrian allusion repurposed mystically).[^3] The poem invokes Sufi themes, declaring the "negation of being" (*fana fi Allah*)—echoing "La ilaha illallah" (There is no god but God)—as the heart's greatest wonder, hidden from the unseeing.[^4] Restless quicksilver transmutes to pure silver in stillness, and love's Abraham shatters the ego's idol, with love's ecstasy (*Tasnim*, a paradisal spring) curing reason's limits.[^5]

The full poem reads:

> Swami Ram Tirath  
> In the river's embrace, O restless drop,  
> you were a pearl, and have now become a pearl beyond price.  
> Ah, with what grace you unveiled the secret of color and scent,  
> while I remain a prisoner of their distinctions.  
> Annihilated, life's clamor became the tumult of Judgment Day;  
> This spark, extinguished, became the fire-temple of Azar.  
> The negation of being is a wonder of the knowing heart;  
> In the river of 'La' lies hidden the pearl of 'illa-Allah'.  
> To the unseeing eye, the final meaning is concealed;  
> When its restlessness ceases, quicksilver is raw silver.  
> The Abraham of Love shatters the idol of the Self;  
> The remedy for reason is the ecstasy of Love's Tasnim.[^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" [p-d1bdd5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-d1bdd5).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" [p-cb1e8e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-cb1e8e).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" [p-420405](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-420405).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" [p-ef7cac](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-ef7cac).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" [p-fc9342](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-fc9342) and [p-7de605](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-7de605).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/).
